metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify or correct the plan's 'NO duplication' claim for the Anthropic path: runner.py:288-289 sets `model_settings["timeout"]` WITHOUT a provider guard, so after this story both the httpx.Timeout AND model_settings['timeout'] will be active on the Anthropic path; the plan should either add an Anthropic-exclusion guard to the model_settings['timeout'] line OR acknowledge that both are set (same value, model_settings is a belt-and-suspenders passthrough that pydantic-ai may ignore for a custom-client model).
findings.finding: The plan claims 'NO duplication with model_settings["timeout"] on the Anthropic path … governs the OTHER providers (OpenAI/Google)' but runner.py:288-289 sets `model_settings["timeout"]` WITHOUT any Anthropic-exclusion guard — it fires for ALL providers including Anthropic. After this story lands, the Anthropic path will have BOTH `httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s, ...)` on the custom client AND `model_settings["timeout"] = float(cfg.timeout_s)` passed to `Agent(...)`. The plan's 'one authoritative mechanism per path, not two competing on one path' narrative is not supported by the code.
findings.location: Plan section 'What' (per-request READ timeout), citing runner.py:288-289; also runner.py:271-291 (model_settings block)
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a provider guard (`if cfg.timeout_s and not resolved.startswith('anthropic'): model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s)`) to make the single-mechanism claim true, or update the plan narrative to state 'both mechanisms are set on the Anthropic path; httpx.Timeout is authoritative at the transport, model_settings["timeout"] is a redundant passthrough that pydantic-ai ignores when a custom httpx client is supplied.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the plan (or a probe in `test_llm_timeouts.py`) that confirms whether `model_settings['timeout']` is applied by pydantic-ai's `AnthropicProvider` when a custom `httpx.AsyncClient` is already set — either cite a pydantic-ai source (e.g. the `AnthropicProvider` source showing it ignores `model_settings.timeout` when `anthropic_client` is supplied) or acknowledge both mechanisms coexist on the Anthropic path with the same value.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that on the Anthropic path, `model_settings['timeout']` is 'a no-op' / 'governs only OTHER providers' once the custom `httpx.AsyncClient` is in place — i.e., that pydantic-ai's Anthropic provider does NOT apply `model_settings['timeout']` to override the already-configured `http_client.timeout`. This is a mechanistic claim about pydantic-ai's internal layering (AnthropicProvider + model_settings interaction) that the plan's offline experiment log does not probe. If pydantic-ai DOES apply `model_settings['timeout']` as a per-call override on top of the client default, both mechanisms would fire simultaneously on the Anthropic path (using the same `cfg.timeout_s` value, so no observable difference today — but a future change to either knob independently would silently contradict the plan's 'one authoritative mechanism per path' claim).
findings.location: What section — 'NO duplication with model_settings[timeout]: on the ANTHROPIC path rebar uses arcticduck's CUSTOM httpx.AsyncClient, so THIS client's httpx.Timeout is the authoritative per-request bound'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, replace the absolute 'NO duplication' claim with: 'On the Anthropic path both model_settings[timeout] and httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s) may fire; they use the same cfg.timeout_s value so behavior is identical today — the httpx-level bound is the VERIFIED one (experiment log); model_settings[timeout] is retained for OpenAI/Google and is harmless on Anthropic at the same value.' This is more accurate and eliminates the unverified mechanistic claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the What/Per-request READ timeout section, acknowledge that `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` already has a `cfg.timeout_s`-based fallback at line 697, and clarify that the plan's change is to pass `httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s, connect=10.0, write=30.0, pool=10.0)` EXPLICITLY at the call site (runner.py:243) to replace the undifferentiated total-timeout fallback with per-component timeouts.
findings.finding: The plan's `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` call site (runner.py:243) is already present and only passes `_name`, `base_url=_direct`, `cfg=cfg` — NO `http_timeout`. The plan correctly identifies this as the edit point. However, the EXISTING fallback at runner.py:697 (`timeout = http_timeout if http_timeout is not None else httpx.Timeout(float(cfg.timeout_s))`) already constructs an `httpx.Timeout` from `cfg.timeout_s` — but as a TOTAL timeout (`httpx.Timeout(float(cfg.timeout_s))` sets all components to the same value, including `read`). The plan's change to `httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s, connect=10.0, write=30.0, pool=10.0)` is a real behavioral delta: the existing code sets TOTAL=`cfg.timeout_s` (all components equal), while the new code sets ONLY `read=cfg.timeout_s` with tighter connect/write/pool. The plan does NOT acknowledge this pre-existing behavior or explain why the current fallback doesn't already achieve the stated goal, which may confuse reviewers into thinking the seam was previously unwired.
findings.location: What / Per-request READ timeout bullet; Scope section; runner.py:697

findings.finding: The 'no new asyncio event loop in the gate path' grep/structural test lacks a specified scope boundary, making it ambiguous given the existing `asyncio.run(_http_client.aclose())` already present in runner.py's teardown `finally` block (line 385). An implementor writing this test must decide whether teardown counts as 'the gate path' — but the plan provides no definition. If the grep matches teardown, the test either false-fails or requires an undocumented carve-out; if it excludes teardown, that boundary rule is nowhere stated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'No total-runtime timeout and no new asyncio event loop are introduced in the gate path (asserted by a grep/structural test)'; also runner.py:385
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, replace 'no new asyncio event loop are introduced in the gate path (asserted by a grep/structural test)' with 'no asyncio event loop or `asyncio.run` call is introduced IN THE AGENTIC RUN PATH (the `try:` body of `PydanticAIRunner.run`); the pre-existing `asyncio.run(_http_client.aclose())` in the `finally` teardown block is explicitly excluded from this assertion. The test asserts this by inspecting the AST or by a targeted grep that excludes the teardown block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify (and implement) whether `model_settings['timeout']` is suppressed for the Anthropic path when the new `httpx.Timeout` is active — specifically confirm that pydantic-ai does NOT forward `model_settings['timeout']` as a per-request `timeout=` override that would collapse the structured `httpx.Timeout(read=..., connect=10.0)` back to a flat scalar, and if it does, gate `model_settings['timeout']` away from the Anthropic path so the client-level `httpx.Timeout` is genuinely authoritative.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the new `httpx.Timeout(read=..., connect=10.0, ...)` on the custom `AsyncClient` is the 'authoritative per-request bound' for the Anthropic path, but `model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s)` is currently set unconditionally (runner.py:288-289) and will remain active for Anthropic calls too. pydantic-ai forwards `model_settings['timeout']` to the Anthropic SDK as a per-request `timeout=` kwarg (e.g. `messages.create(timeout=<float>)`), which in httpx overrides the client-level `httpx.Timeout` for that specific request. The plan's 'one authoritative mechanism per path' claim is only correct if `model_settings['timeout']` is suppressed (or gated away) for the Anthropic path — otherwise the structured `httpx.Timeout` object on the client is silently overridden by the flat float on every actual API call, and the differentiated `connect=10.0` / `read=cfg.timeout_s` granularity is lost.
findings.location: What section — per-request READ timeout mechanism; runner.py:243 (call site) and runner.py:288-289 (model_settings['timeout'] wiring)
findings.suggested_fix: In runner.py, gate the `model_settings['timeout']` assignment to non-Anthropic providers: `if cfg.timeout_s and not resolved.startswith('anthropic'): model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s)`. For Anthropic, the `httpx.Timeout` on the custom `AsyncClient` is the sole authority; the model_settings scalar is redundant at best and a silent override at worst. Add a probe in `test_llm_timeouts.py` that asserts the differentiated timeout values (connect=10.0, read=cfg.timeout_s) are preserved on the actual httpx client rather than collapsed to a scalar.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit decision rule (in the What section or AC2) specifying whether `model_settings['timeout']` should be suppressed/removed for the Anthropic path when the new `httpx.Timeout` is active, or confirm with a code comment that pydantic-ai's `AnthropicModel` ignores `model_settings['timeout']` when the transport already enforces the bound.
findings.finding: The plan introduces httpx.Timeout on the Anthropic-path AsyncClient and claims 'one authoritative mechanism per path, not two competing on one path' — but never specifies a decision rule for the existing model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s) that is currently set unconditionally for ALL providers including Anthropic (runner.py:288-289). The two mechanisms operate at different SDK layers (pydantic-ai ModelSettings timeout vs. httpx transport read timeout). The plan does not say whether to suppress model_settings['timeout'] for the Anthropic path, leave it in place, or explain why co-existence is harmless. An implementer has no authoritative rule to follow.
findings.location: What section / runner.py:288-289 / AC line 2 ('httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s, …) is passed into _build_retrying_anthropic_model')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section: 'On the Anthropic path, model_settings["timeout"] is suppressed (not set) when http_timeout is provided, because the httpx.AsyncClient's httpx.Timeout is the authoritative bound at the transport layer; model_settings["timeout"] continues to govern OpenAI/Google paths unchanged.' Alternatively, verify and document that pydantic-ai passes model_settings['timeout'] to the Anthropic SDK which ignores it when the underlying httpx client already has a timeout — and add a comment to that effect in runner.py.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ensure `docs/config.md` explicitly documents that `timeout_s` serves dual roles by provider path — `httpx.Timeout.read` on the Anthropic path (custom httpx client) and `model_settings['timeout']` on OpenAI/Google paths (pydantic-ai's internal client) — so operators understand why the mechanism differs.
findings.finding: The docs/config.md update is specified to 'document both' (cfg.timeout_s reuse and llm_tool_timeout_s), but the plan does not explicitly call for documenting the provider-path split: cfg.timeout_s now drives httpx.Timeout.read on the Anthropic path AND model_settings['timeout'] on the OpenAI/Google path — two different mechanisms, same config key. Without a clear prose note in docs/config.md, operators on non-Anthropic providers may be confused about why the read-timeout wiring behaves differently or why their custom httpx client is not affected.
findings.location: Scope section / docs/config.md update bullet; 'document both in docs/config.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Provider-path note' callout in docs/config.md under the timeout_s entry: 'On the Anthropic provider path, this value is applied as the HTTP read timeout on rebar's shared httpx.AsyncClient (connect=10 s, write=30 s are fixed). On OpenAI/Google paths, it is passed as model_settings["timeout"] to pydantic-ai's internally-built client.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the plan title to reflect actual deliverables: e.g., rename to 'Activity-based liveness: per-request timeout + step caps (async idle-watchdog deferred to ADR)' so title and body agree on scope without requiring the reader to reconcile them.
findings.finding: The plan title ('per-request timeout + step caps + idle-watchdog') implies the async idle-watchdog is a deliverable of this story, while the body explicitly states it is deferred to ADR content only — a conflicting signal an executor reading the title could misinterpret as scope.
findings.location: Plan title vs. 'Deferred (ADR content only, NOT built)' section and AC #5
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the title to: 'Activity-based liveness: per-request READ timeout + per-tool timeout + step caps (async idle-watchdog: ADR only, not built)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the plan title (or add a prominent subtitle/callout) to make clear the idle-watchdog is deferred to ADR content only — e.g. 'Activity-based liveness: per-request timeout + step caps (idle-watchdog deferred to ADR)'.
findings.finding: The plan title lists 'idle-watchdog' as a positive, co-equal mechanism alongside 'per-request timeout' and 'step caps', but the body explicitly defers the watchdog to ADR content only (not built). A reader of the title alone would expect a watchdog implementation to ship in this ticket.
findings.location: Title: 'Activity-based liveness: per-request timeout + step caps + idle-watchdog (no total-runtime timeout)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'Activity-based liveness: per-request timeout + step caps (async idle-watchdog deferred — ADR only)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope section that `tool_timeout=cfg.llm_tool_timeout_s` is injected into the TOP-LEVEL `kwargs` dict (runner.py ~line 250-254, alongside `system_prompt`/`tools`/`toolsets`) — NOT into `model_settings` — so that `**kwargs` correctly delivers it to `Agent.__init__`.
findings.finding: The plan says `tool_timeout=cfg.llm_tool_timeout_s` is added to 'the shared `Agent(...)` kwargs (flows to all three `Agent(model, **kwargs)` sites)'. However, `tool_timeout` is an `Agent.__init__` constructor parameter — it must be added to the `kwargs` dict that is unpacked via `**kwargs` at construction time. The plan does not explicitly state WHERE in `run()` the `tool_timeout` key is injected into `kwargs`, and the `kwargs` dict is built at line 250 BEFORE `model_settings` is assembled. If the implementer adds `tool_timeout` to `model_settings` (wrong) rather than the top-level `kwargs` dict (correct), it will be silently ignored or raise a type error. The plan's shorthand 'shared `Agent(...)` kwargs' is ambiguous between the `kwargs` dict and `model_settings`.
findings.location: What section / Scope section: 'add `tool_timeout=cfg.llm_tool_timeout_s` to the shared `Agent(...)` construction kwargs'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet, add: 'Add `tool_timeout=cfg.llm_tool_timeout_s` to the `kwargs` dict at the point it is constructed in `PydanticAIRunner.run()` (alongside `system_prompt`, `tools`, `toolsets`), before the `Agent(model, **kwargs)` call sites.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test) that drives a MockTransport which always sleeps past the read timeout, asserts `httpx.TimeoutException` triggers the retry transport exactly `llm_retry_max_attempts` times, and that the final raised exception is `LLMUnavailableError` (not a raw `httpx.TimeoutException`), pinning the retry-to-classification chain.
findings.finding: The plan makes a completion-relevant behavioral claim — 'a transiently-slow model is retried; a PERSISTENTLY hung model times out each attempt, exhausts llm_retry_max_attempts, and surfaces LLMUnavailableError' — but no AC or probe exercises the retry-transport + httpx.TimeoutException → LLMUnavailableError classification chain.
findings.location: What section: 'Interaction with the retry transport (correct, not a bug)...' and Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers this claim)
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_llm_timeouts.py`, add a test: construct the full retry-transport chain (as built by `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`) with a MockTransport that always times out; assert (a) the transport is called exactly `llm_retry_max_attempts` times, and (b) the exception raised to the caller is `LLMUnavailableError`. This pins the claim 'bounded either way' with evidence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a clause to AC1 explicitly verifying that `docs/config.md` documents `REBAR_LLM_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S` and the reused `cfg.timeout_s` read-timeout behavior (so the deliverable listed in Scope/Files has a covering criterion).
findings.finding: docs/config.md update is described in Scope and Files but has no covering acceptance criterion — an implementer could skip it without violating any AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 to read: '…`REBAR_LLM_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S` in `test_config_llm.py`'s `_clean` fixture; `docs/config.md` updated to document both `llm_tool_timeout_s` and the per-request read timeout reuse of `timeout_s`; `docs/env-vars.md` regenerated (drift gate passes).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document `connect=10.0`, `write=30.0`, `pool=10.0` as intentionally fixed HTTP sub-timeouts in `docs/config.md` (a brief note is sufficient), and consider whether at least `connect` warrants a config knob (e.g., `REBAR_LLM_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_S`) given that connect failures are environment-sensitive.
findings.finding: The sub-values of httpx.Timeout — connect=10.0, write=30.0, pool=10.0 — are hardcoded constants in the plan with no config knob and no documentation as intentionally fixed architectural choices. While these are standard HTTP plumbing defaults, connect=10.0 s in particular could be too tight in high-latency or proxy environments, and operators have no recourse without a code change.
findings.location: What section / Scope section: 'httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s, connect=10.0, write=30.0, pool=10.0)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability (at minimum a structured log warning, ideally a metric increment) when `tool_timeout` cancels an async tool, so operators can detect MCP/async tool degradation without waiting for run failures.
findings.finding: Tool-timeout cancellation events are entirely silent — no log or metric is emitted when a tool is cancelled by tool_timeout. The plan states the timeout 'raises NO exception into the runner' and 'the model simply receives a tool error and proceeds', with no instrumentation call-out. Operators cannot distinguish a run where tools timed out repeatedly (indicating MCP/async tool degradation) from a healthy run.
findings.location: What section — per-TOOL timeout bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC3; Files: tests/unit/test_llm_timeouts.py
findings.suggested_fix: In the pydantic-ai tool error callback path (or by wrapping the Agent call site), emit `log.warning('tool_timeout_fired', tool_name=..., elapsed=...)` and increment a counter metric (e.g., `rebar_tool_timeout_total`). Reference this instrumentation in the AC and in `runner.py` file scope.

findings.finding: The experiment log included in the plan demonstrates `httpx.Timeout(600.0, read=300.0, connect=10.0).read == 300.0` — a constructor form with an explicit `read=` kwarg — but the Scope section specifies `httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s, connect=10.0)` (positional total + connect only, no explicit `read=`). More critically, the plan claims the `ReadTimeout`-fires-under-`run_sync` behavior is 'VERIFIED by arcticduck's spike', but the experiment log does not include a sleeping-server → ReadTimeout probe; that probe is listed in the Verification section as a TEST TO BE WRITTEN, not as a completed offline spike. The critical capability assertion — that a `read` timeout fires at the httpx transport level during `agent.run_sync()` with the specific constructor form used — is referenced but not shown in the evidence log.
findings.location: ## Verified library behavior (experiment log) / ## What — Per-request READ timeout / ## Scope
findings.suggested_fix: In the experiment log, add a line such as: `MockTransport(sleep=2s) + httpx.Timeout(5.0, connect=10.0) → Agent.run_sync() → httpx.ReadTimeout raised at ~5s` (or use the `_wrapped_transport` seam from the existing test). In the Scope, also clarify whether the intent is `httpx.Timeout(connect=10.0, read=cfg.timeout_s)` (explicit read) or the positional-total form — both give `read=cfg.timeout_s` but the distinction avoids ambiguity for the implementor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live-path gate (or explicitly document its deferral with rationale in a named child): at minimum, add an integration-test AC (even if skipped in CI by default) that constructs the real `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` with a short httpx.Timeout and a local echo server that delays past it, confirming `httpx.ReadTimeout` fires on the actual Anthropic client construction path — not a MockTransport that bypasses client construction.
findings.finding: The httpx.Timeout wiring in `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` is an always-on production cutover to a new code path (real Anthropic HTTP calls), but every AC exercises it via MockTransport only — no AC exercises the changed path against the live Anthropic endpoint after the merge.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #2: 'a sleeping MockTransport trips httpx.ReadTimeout under run_sync (probe), and a just-under-timeout MockTransport completes normally'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'An integration probe constructs `_build_retrying_anthropic_model(http_timeout=httpx.Timeout(read=0.1, connect=5.0, write=5.0, pool=5.0))` and points it at a local TCP server that accepts but never responds; assert `httpx.ReadTimeout` (or `LLMUnavailableError`) is raised within ~0.5s — confirming the timeout is wired through the actual Anthropic client, not only a mock transport.' Mark `@pytest.mark.integration` and skip in default CI with a rationale comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback note to the plan (e.g., in Scope or a Risk section) stating that the back-out path for the new httpx read timeout enforcement is to raise `REBAR_TIMEOUT_S` / `cfg.timeout_s` or revert the `http_timeout` wiring in `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`, so T4's reversibility requirement is met by more than implicit config tunability.
findings.finding: The plan adds an enforced httpx read timeout on the Anthropic path (a behavioral change — calls that previously succeeded by exceeding `cfg.timeout_s` will now fail) but provides no explicit rollback or back-out mechanism; acknowledging the change is intentional does not satisfy T4's requirement for a named reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: What section (per-request READ timeout) / Scope (NOT in scope) / AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rollback note: 'Rollback: revert `_build_retrying_anthropic_model(http_timeout=...)` call-site to pass `None` (disables the explicit read timeout) or raise `REBAR_TIMEOUT_S` to accommodate slower responses; no data migration required.'

findings.finding: AC2 references 'both modes' without defining what the two modes are, leaving an executor to infer this from the codebase rather than from the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'httpx.Timeout(cfg.timeout_s, connect=10.0) is passed into _build_retrying_anthropic_model(http_timeout=...) for both modes'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'for both modes' with 'for both the single-turn and agentic/multi-turn call paths in runner.py (wherever _build_retrying_anthropic_model is invoked)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
